original_abstract: Establishing a clear formation mechanism for carbon dots (CDs) remains a significant challenge in nanotechnology. In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal treatment of 4-aminoantipyrine, yields blue-fluorescent nanoparticles (~5 nm) with high water solubility and gram-scale productivity. The structural evolution and growth mechanisms were systematically monitored across various reaction times (3 to 21 hours) using FT-IR, $^{13}$C-NMR, $^1$H-NMR, and UV-visible spectroscopy. Spectroscopic evidence reveals that extended hydrothermal treatment leads to the degradation of the precursor’s aromatic signals and the simultaneous emergence of aliphatic and carbonyl functional groups. This transformation is driven by the hydrolysis of the pyrazole ring and noncovalent $\pi$–$\pi$ stacking interactions of aromatic residues, which facilitate core formation. As the reaction progresses, the surface becomes increasingly enriched with polar functional groups, resulting in a significant enhancement of the photoluminescent quantum yield. HR-TEM and XRD analysis confirm the production of high-purity NCDs with an amorphous turbostratic carbon phase and a lattice d-spacing of 0.26 nm. These insights provide a deeper understanding of how synthesis duration dictates the chemical and optical properties of NCDs, offering a cost-effective pathway for optimizing high-quality carbonaceous nanomaterials.
humanized_abstract: The formation mechanism of carbon dots (CDs) remains unclear. Here, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) are fabricated via hydrothermal reaction of 4-aminoantipyrine with the yields of blue emitting nanoparticles ( ~ 5 nm) with high water solubility and quantity in gram scale. By analyzing FT-IR, $^{13}$C-NMR, $^1$H-NMR and UV–visible spectra at different hydrothermal reaction time points of 3, 9 and 21 h in details, the structural evolution and growth mechanism of precursor toward as-prepared NCDs were thoroughly discussed. Spectroscopic results clearly demonstrate that the primary structure of precursor was decomposed to yield new spectral signals attributed to aliphatic and carbonyl functional groups through hydrolyzing the pyrazole ring of precursor and breaking down the noncovalent $\pi$–$\pi$ stacking interactions between aromatic rings for forming CDs core. Meanwhile, the increased surface functional groups with hydrothermal reaction time significantly improved the photoluminescent quantum yield (PLQY) of CDs. The HR-TEM and XRD results demonstrate that high purity of as-fabricated NCDs were with amorphous turbostratic carbon structure and lattice distance of 0.26 nm. Overall, understanding the structural evolution and growth mechanism of precursor toward the CDs and affecting factors of different reaction time, present a facile method to fabricate high quality CDs using nitrogen rich carbon precursor.
